step1.title: Welcome to DayStep
step1.body: A daily-life companion designed for adult ADHD.
step1.rationale: We'll walk you through the main screens in under a minute. You can skip any time.

step2.title: Today's progress
step2.body: See completed and remaining tasks at a glance.
step2.rationale: ADHD brains rely on visualized progress to stay motivated. The large progress ring lets even small wins register.

step3.title: Today's mission
step3.body: Pick one thing to focus on first.
step3.rationale: One thing at a time — multitasking is the heaviest load on an ADHD brain. Narrowing to a single mission lowers the start barrier.

step4.title: Daily care
step4.body: Sleep, ADHD basics, and tidying — three core axes.
step4.rationale: Sleep, focus, and a tidy environment are the three load-bearing axes of ADHD daily life. They live in one place so the flow doesn't break.

step5.title: Five tabs at the bottom
step5.body: Home, Planner, Focus, Notes, More — that's it.
step5.rationale: Too many options trigger decision paralysis. Five tabs keeps the choice simple.

references.title: References
references.intro: ADHD-related content in DayStep draws on the following peer-reviewed academic sources.
items: [1] Hvolby, A. (2015). Associations of sleep disturbance with ADHD: implications for treatment. ADHD Attention Deficit and Hyperactivity Disorders, 7(1), 1–18.
[2] Coogan, A. N. & McGowan, N. M. (2017). A systematic review of circadian function, chronotype and chronotherapy in ADHD. ADHD Attention Deficit and Hyperactivity Disorders, 9(3), 129–147.
[3] Volkow, N. D. et al. (2012). Evidence that sleep deprivation downregulates dopamine D2R in ventral striatum in the human brain. Journal of Neuroscience, 32(19), 6711–6717.
[4] Kooij, J. J. S. & Bijlenga, D. (2013). The circadian rhythm in adult attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder: current state of affairs. Expert Review of Neurotherapeutics, 13(10), 1107–1116.

disclaimer.title: Educational use
disclaimer.body: This content is provided for educational and self-management support only. It does not replace professional medical diagnosis or treatment. If difficulties with sleep, attention, or daily functioning persist, consult a psychiatrist or qualified healthcare provider.
